# Transcrição: 01 How to Audit an Account

- **Fonte:** 01 How to Audit an Account
- **Duração:** 31:50
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Alright guys, in today's training I'm going to take you through auditing your leads in your clients. This is a really, really, really important step. I feel like I say that about every single training because it is. But you get this wrong and look, here's what happens. You're not going to set the right expectations. The client is going to get disappointed. Things are going to get ugly. They're not going to pay you. It can lead to some really, really bad situations in your agency. So understanding how to price yourself properly and understanding whether or not to even take on a client is a really, really important step of running a successful Facebook ads business. If you get this wrong, you're going to underscore yourself. You're going to set the wrong expectations or you're going to onboard the wrong type of clients into your business. And that is never going to lead to a good outcome for you or for your team. So this is a really, really important part of running a successful Facebook ads business. So why do we audit a client? So as you already know at this stage in the academy, what we do is we use a two-call process. So a two-call close essentially. We don't close on the first call simply because these are cold leads and it's a pretty blunt funnel that we use. So depending on the funnel that you're using, it's typically very straight to the point and the audience is quite cold. And obviously part of that strategy behind a two-call close is that we want to warm up that lead so that when we pitch our services, there's rapport, there's trust, and the lead has known us a little bit longer to handle that price point that we need to pitch at. But of course the second part of that is that we need to do our due diligence. We need to research this lead and to see are they actually converting the way that they are saying they're converting? Are there potential issues that I need to bring up with this client before I can even work with them? And are they even ready for Facebook ads? Can they even handle Facebook ads? What's the point of driving Facebook ads to a client that can only handle one client per month if you're not going to make any money off of that, right? So you need to make sure you properly audit that client. So the main reasons for this is we're identifying their assets and we're looking at their funnel. Is it a validated funnel? Is it viable? Can I work with this or does it need a brand new build? And then setting the right expectations. So look, your website is converting at this, it should be converting at this, this is going to be a problem. Instead of mentioning that two months down the track when it simply looks like a cop out when you do that. And then of course protecting yourself as well as the client. You don't want to say yes to everyone.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

That is not good business. You want to make sure that you're only onboarding clients that are a right fit for you, that you have rapport with, that you feel you're going to make a great team and add value. So it needs to be reciprocal. Do not take on clients just because you need the money. I guarantee you 1000% that will always backfire on you. So if you know someone's not a good fit or you're not sure or you're really doubting that this will work and you take them on pretty much every single time that will backfire. So don't do it. Don't even go there. Save yourself the money. So the main reason that we ask is the client ready is obviously in Facebook there's funnels. We're working with funnels, right? So whether or not they're lead gen, e-commerce or coaching client, there's going to be some kind of funnel that out there that essentially tries to go out, identify the leads and drive them through into action. So that could be a purchase. It could be booking a call or it could be simply giving them their email and phone number. So this is an example of the Facebook funnel that Facebook provides. So taking them from awareness, consideration, acquisition and then retention. This is a pretty standard funnel and something that I highly encourage you guys all to work towards. So what you're trying to assess when you're looking at a client or lead is to go, do they have the right tools, the assets and the systems in place and have they proven that they're able to go out there and take someone from the awareness stage all the way through to the retention transaction phase? Is that the case? And I'm going to show you some tools and some questions to ask here, but this is really important and that's why we're doing this. If a client has not proven that they can do this, essentially what you have in front of you is an unvalidated funnel and sometimes even an unvalidated product or a business, if it's a very new concept. And then knowing this allows you to set the right expectations and come into the relationship under those expectations where you can clearly tell the client, look, this is an unvalidated product, an unvalidated business that you have, it's a new thing. We're not guaranteeing results, but here's what we can do. So understanding that is really, really important. Where are they at in their journey? What do they have going on for them? And are they ready for Facebook ads? Have they made it work for them? What's the health of what they have or what they're sitting on at the moment? So some of the things that we want to audit and be sure to download the template. There's a template provided below this training video and just download that and work through that with every single lead and you will get really, really good. Eventually you'll almost be able to pick all of this up right on the phone call.

## [06:02 - 09:03]

In fact, I encourage you that on the first call you have this sheet with you that you can sort of start to pre-fill. Now be careful, I don't want you to read off of this sheet, I don't want it to sound scripted, but certainly you can pull inspiration for some of the questions and definitely it's something that you can fill while you're on the call with them. So the things that you want to look at is their website. So I'm going to take you through some of the different questions or things to look at with their website. But clearly for certain types of funnels and clients such as e-commerce, a website plays a fundamental part in that funnel and it's critical that it converts the way it needs to convert. We're going to look at their stock levels and scalability. So this is something that Tom talks about sometimes with one of his clients that is constantly going out of stock. It can be very difficult when you're running ads and your client's running out of stock, how scalable are they, how many calls can they handle, how many sales can they handle. What is the potential for your growth if you take this client on? Not in the next month, but in the next six months. If you need to be earning at least 5, 10K from a client per month, how many sales would they need to generate in order for them to afford that fee and are they even able to cope with that type of scale? They're messaging. When they're explaining to you what they do and their services, does it make sense? If they struggle to explain what they do or what they offer, I guarantee you you're going to get yourself in a bad situation because you can't have effective marketing if the client doesn't clearly know what they do or what they offer. It's almost impossible, right? So if they can't explain it to you clearly and it doesn't make sense to you, that means you're going to put yourself in a position where now you're going to be rejigging their marketing for them. You're going to be helping them on their marketing strategy, developing a clear USP. And that's a whole different kettle of fish, right? So be careful about that. What systems and platforms do they use? This is something a lot of us overlook, but it's really important. For example, I'll give you an example here. In Eight Loop, we do not work with Infusionsoft. We just don't have anyone on the team that is certified in Infusionsoft and you need a certification to use Infusionsoft. It's that confusing, right? So for us, if we have clients that come to us with Infusionsoft, well, we either tell them we need to shift the system or we're going to be contracting this out. And we're going to need to make sure that that's reflected in the fees. So be careful. Now, you don't necessarily have to tell your client that you're contracting that out, but internally you need to understand that, hey, if this client is on Infusionsoft or

on a platform that I don't have knowledge or my team doesn't have knowledge, it means we're going to have to bring on a contractor which eats into our profit margin. So make sure you carefully consider this. Same goes with e-commerce. What e-commerce platform are they on? Are they on Wix? I guarantee you that's a problem. So you need to shift them onto a proper website. So think about the tools and systems that you and your team are comfortable working with and make sure you ask your client what tools and platforms that they're on or systems that they're using. What's their content like? Do they have content that you can pull? Have they been in the press? Have they done influencer marketing? Do they have great videos or graphics or images? Or are you going to be creating everything from scratch? Are there images of the client themselves if that's relevant? Are there any ads already created? What is the extent of their content library? Can you use a lot of this stuff? Is it kind of really bad? Is it neat a whole rejig? Is it way off topic? Is there branding off even, right? So that goes back a little bit to their messaging. The response time. This is really important as well if you're in the service sector space. So if you're, for example, driving leads to a realtor or a gym or a Cairo, how long do they take to get in touch with those leads? Now you may want to secret shop this because a lot of clients are going to bullshit you here. They're going to say, oh yeah, yeah, we get back within a couple hours. When in reality it's about three days. So don't take their word for it. I promise you that most of them have no freaking idea or they are so deluded or they're not paying attention enough that they don't actually know what the real response time is. So what is the response time? If you go and run a lead gen campaign to a client that takes a week to get in touch with leads, I guarantee you he's going to struggle to close those leads. And guess what's going to happen? He's going to turn around and say Facebook ads don't work. It's not going to be a long term solution. So you either need to address that from the get go and make them aware that they need to address this and that this is a problem and if they don't address it within a certain period of time, you won't be able to continue to work with them so that they know there's responsibility. They take responsibility and it's their own us to fix it. Again, you're doing that from the get go so that it doesn't sound like a cop out if it's a discussion you're having three months later, right? What is their sales skills, their conversion rates? This is important especially when you're working with high ticket coaches or service sector clients. What conversion rate do they have on the call with clients? Have they ever been on a call with a cold audience before?

## [12:07 - 15:12]

A lot of these guys like mortgage lenders have only ever spoken to referrals or word of mouth. That's how they build their business. So they'll tell you on the phone, oh yes, my conversion rate is 90%. When an actual fact, they've never actually been on a phone with a cold lead and their conversion rate is probably going to be more around 10%. So resetting those expectations right from the get go. Making them understand, okay, here's the thing, you've never really been on a call with a cold lead, is that right? Yes. Okay, so I know you're converting at 90% but Facebook leads are going to convert a lot lower because they're cold. Can you see how that might happen? And that's going to be around 10%. So saying that from the get go protects you. You don't want to have a situation where two months in the client goes, I'm only 10% of these or you know, it's way lower than it was. What's going on? Facebook ads are crap, right? When in actual fact, it's great. Same thing with e-commerce. What is the conversion rate of their existing website? If you can get access to their Google analytics to view this, do so. It's really, really important. E-commerce should be converting at around 2%. That's really normal. If a website's converting significantly lower, that's something you need to address with them, it's going to cost you a lot more to generate a sale. And then what are their goals and their objectives? How hungry are they, right? And how realistic are those as well? So what are their goals and their objectives? Are they only trying to make $10,000 a month? That's going to be a problem, right, if you're trying to scale and make $10,000 a month from your clients. They're not going to pay you that if that's what they're making. So what are their goals and how hungry, and what's their desire behind that, right? How hungry did they want that? Those are really, really fundamental questions that you need to run through with the lead. And most of this is stuff that you're going to be asking during your discovery call, that first phone call phase. And of course, as I mentioned, don't take their word for it on every single one of those. Check it out yourself. So mystery shop them. Put yourself through the funnel. If it's a very expensive product, ask them to give you access to it and sign an NDA, right? So some of the tools that we use to run audits on clients is obviously the Facebook Pixel Helper. So make sure you have that on a Chrome browser. Go on their landing pages, on their website. Do they have the Pixel? Are they using events? Is schema installed properly? So that's part of the new micro data and the Pixel. Is it installed correctly? Is it reporting back to Facebook correctly? Now if it's not, you need to pull in a developer to set that up and you need to tell the client that from the get go so it doesn't surprise them a month then, right? You can also see how fast landing page load, so using tools like PageSpeed Insights. Obviously the speed at which a page loads is really going to impact how relevant or

## [15:12 - 18:13]

the quality, the quality part of the algorithm in the auction process and your ad results, right? And also it's going to impact conversion rates significantly. We also use the Debugger tool. Now a word of warning here, it's going to tell you a lot of developer slang as well. So tap into a developer if you're not sure what some of the stuff means. You can also use Google Analytics. And look guys, if you have no idea what you're looking at and if analytics and data is not your strength, tap into someone that does. It's really important that you do this. If you audit a client properly, it will protect you and again it will protect the client later on. So typically a quick rundown and I'm going to show you in a second in an ad account of an ad account audit is first we request access and typically sometimes they'll need an NDA for that and that's okay. So we request access as an analyst to the ad account. If you can do that on the first phone call with them, that's ideal because sometimes customers don't know how to find notifications and it can take a week or two just to get access. So if you can do it while you're screen sharing, that's the ideal situation on the first call. The first thing that we do once we have access to that is we look at the events that they're using to track. And so we're looking under the pixel tab and I'm going to show you in a second. And then we're also going to look under the custom conversion tab and try to see what events they're tracking. There's nothing in either of those that's a problem that means they haven't tracked anything at all. It pretty much means that there's nothing for you to look at in that ad account at this stage because you're pretty much running blind and they were running blind as well. So they never optimize things properly and they didn't know where things were coming from, what it was costing them, how to optimize, what was working, what was not. As I mentioned before, is schema installed correctly and again I'll show you that in a minute. Have they set up any custom audiences? So go under the audience tab and see if there's any custom audiences and that's going to give you an indication if they're using retargeting. And that's a promising sign if they are, right? If not, then that's a massive win.

## [18:13 - 21:15]

They haven't done that. Is there event tracking? So when you find out if there's standard events or custom conversions being used, bring that up in the reporting columns and then check the health of that. Was there pockets that they missed? Was it fed to desktop, mobile? Break it down by ages, by genders, by countries. Try to find the wins and the pockets. Look at their click-through rates and the creative. Is it really bad? Is the relevant score really low? These are all going to be indications on whether or not the funnel is healthy. If it is fantastic, if it's not, then that's not necessarily a bad thing. It means that there's a lot of wins. What you would be nervous of is if you go into an ad account and everything looks great, but they're not getting the results that they need. ## [30:26 - 31:50]

So making sure that that gets installed properly on the client's website. So there's a checklist below that you can use. There's the audit process, the sheet that you can use as well.
